body:
### Description

## Summary

When using the built-in `angularls` configuration with Angular Language Server installed globally via pnpm on Linux, the generated probe locations may incorrectly resolve to the user's home directory (`$HOME`) instead of a valid global `node_modules` directory.

This appears to happen because the configuration derives probe locations from the `ngserver` executable path and assumes the executable resides within a package directory structure. For pnpm global installations on Linux, `ngserver` is a shell wrapper script, causing the computed probe location to become `$HOME`.

## Environment

* Neovim: 0.12.3
* OS: Linux
* Node version manager: fnm
* Package manager: pnpm
* Angular Language Server: installed globally

```bash
pnpm add -g @angular/language-server
```

Example:

```bash
which ngserver
```

returns:

```text
/home/user/.local/share/pnpm/ngserver
```

The executable is a shell script generated by pnpm.

## Current behavior

The generated command contains probe locations similar to:

```text
--tsProbeLocations /path/to/project/node_modules,/home/user
```

and

```text
--ngProbeLocations /path/to/project/node_modules/@angular/language-server/node_modules,/home/user/@angular/language-server/node_modules
```

The second probe location for `--tsProbeLocations` resolves to the user's home directory rather than a global `node_modules` location.

## Investigation

The issue appears to originate from:

```lua
local ngserver_exe = fn.exepath('ngserver')
local realpath = uv.fs_realpath(ngserver_exe) or ngserver_exe
realpath = resolve_cmd_shim(realpath)
local candidate = fs.normalize(fs.joinpath(fs.dirname(realpath), '../../..'))
```

For a global pnpm installation:

```text
/home/user/.local/share/pnpm/ngserver
```

`resolve_cmd_shim()` returns the same path because it only handles Windows `.cmd` wrappers.

As a result:

```text
dirname(realpath)
/home/user/.local/share/pnpm
```

and

```text
normalize(dirname(realpath) .. "/../../..")
```

becomes:

```text
/home/user
```

which is then added as a probe location.

## Expected behavior

The configuration should derive a valid global package location for pnpm installations (or avoid adding invalid probe locations).

At minimum, probe location discovery should not resolve to `$HOME` when `ngserver` is installed globally via pnpm on Linux.

## Reproduction

1. Install Angular Language Server globally using pnpm:

```bash
pnpm add -g @angular/language-server
```

2. Use the built-in `angularls` configuration.
3. Open an Angular project.
4. Inspect the generated `--tsProbeLocations` / `--ngProbeLocations`.
5. Observe that `$HOME` is used as a probe location.

comment_data.body:
the js-related configs, especially angular, are very complex. PR welcome if it doesn't make things a lot more complicated...

(please check previous PRs/commits to see the reasoning for past decisions)

title: angularls: probe location resolution for global pnpm installations on Linux resolves to $HOME
